title: Almond biscotti
subtitle: Twice-baked Italian almond biscuits for coffee or dessert wine
excerpt: A log of almond-studded dough is baked until firm, sliced on the diagonal, then returned to the oven for a second bake that drives out the remaining moisture. The result is an extremely crisp, dry biscuit with a long shelf life. No butter in the dough: the eggs alone bind the mixture. Best eaten dipped briefly in coffee or Vin Santo.
sourceNotes: Biscotti (singular biscotto) means twice-cooked in Italian. The Tuscan version, cantucci or cantuccini di Prato, made with whole almonds, eggs, flour, and sugar without butter or fat, has been documented in its current form since at least the 17th century. Mattei of Prato, established 1858, is credited with codifying the contemporary Prato recipe. In Britain biscotti became widely available through Italian delis from the 1980s and home recipes have appeared in British baking books since the 1990s. The recipe here is based on the classic cantucci formula: whole or roughly chopped blanched almonds in an egg-and-flour dough.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked biscotti for up to 3 months. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Biscotti improve after a day as the crumb firms further. Keep in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to 4 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 150°C. First bake at 170°C for 25 minutes; second bake after slicing at 150°C conventional (130°C fan) for 20 to 25 minutes until fully dry. The second temperature is lower to dry without burning.

recipeTools.notes: two baking trays, lined with baking paper

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique that defines biscotti: baking the dough first as a log until firm, slicing it on the diagonal while warm, then returning the slices to the oven at a lower temperature to dry them completely. The second bake drives off all residual moisture, producing a hard, shelf-stable biscuit. The drying stage (not the initial bake) is what creates the characteristic crunch. Cutting the log while warm prevents crumbling; cutting cold biscotti tends to shatter them.

content.text: is what creates the shelf life: fully dried biscotti keep for weeks in an airtight tin. The dough is sticky and should be shaped with wet hands on the baking tray rather than rolled. The log spreads slightly during the first bake and that is expected. Cut it while still warm; a serrated knife in a sawing motion prevents crumbling.

Heat the oven to 170°C conventional (150°C fan) and line two baking trays with baking paper. Whisk together the flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt in a large bowl. Beat the eggs with the vanilla and almond extract, then pour into the flour mixture and stir until a sticky dough forms. Fold in the almonds. The dough will be quite stiff.

With damp hands, shape the dough into two logs about 30 × 6 cm on the lined trays. The logs should be about 3 cm high. Bake for 25 minutes until lightly golden and firm to the touch. Remove from the oven and leave to cool for 15 minutes. Reduce the oven to 150°C conventional (130°C fan).

While still warm, cut each log on the diagonal into 1 cm slices using a serrated knife with a sawing motion. Lay the slices cut-side down on the trays. Return to the oven for 20 to 25 minutes, turning once halfway through, until dry and golden on both sides. Cool completely on a rack. They will harden further as they cool.
